videos.title: Adobe Executives Explain What the Market Is Missing
videos.transcript: Hello everyone and welcome back. We're here at Adobe Summit with yet again Hannah from Adobe. Welcome. >> Thank you for having me. >> It's been a while. We we talked at a couple of months ago Adobe Max. >> Yes. >> Since then, I assume a lot has happened >> and AI time. It's been seven years already. >> Yes, maybe more. >> Maybe more. >> Um you're in charge of Genai new business ventures. Yes. Now, before we talk about uh Firefly Foundry and everything that's been happening, can you explain what that means, what your role means? >> I think I have the best job at Adobe, the most fun, because I'm always able to stay on the cutting edge from a new business ventures, all the organic things that we do. So, uh, as you've heard, AI is a thing for the last couple of years. >> Apparently, there's this guy Jensen. >> Yes, we'll talk about that in a second because he's he's a fan of Foundry. Um but we a couple of years ago um we needed to focus on the new business ventures in the geni space and so I'm I think of myself as an entrepreneur um at Adobe which is really nice because I don't have to fundra I but I do have to pitch internally. Okay. And so a couple of years ago uh we launched the um Firefly for enterprise products which include creative production custom models Firefly services which has become really a very strong high growth business for us. Then last year we brought thirdparty models >> into the ecosystem and then this year as we talked about at Max we launched Firefly Foundry. So continuing on our um ethos of really having responsibility in the market but also giving our enterprise customers what they need. >> Okay, fair enough. Is there anything since Max, is there anything that you've seen from a customer standpoint, some feedback, what they've been using, maybe some results, early results? I know there is NBC Universal that's here, Home Depot. What's what's the feedback you got? >> Yeah. So um here uh we have customers obviously foundry from Paramount from NBCU as you said um our partnership with Nvidia launch that's new Walt Disney Imagineering is also here Coca-Cola is also here so I think what we have seen since we last talked is that brands really it resonates with Fortune 2000 companies who care about their brands in a world where technology is moving very fast. talked about how quickly the whirlwind of AI is happening. You want to stay ahead. You don't want to miss a large tectonic shift in technology, but you have to be thoughtful. And I think we heard that in spades from Shanu, from Jensen, from the customers on stage, which is be thoughtful about how you're approaching it. And in that sense, you know, they they vote with their uh customer trust and they trust Adobe as a platform that has figured out multiple moves to the cloud, you know, with digital um the whole idea of just having digital creativity in Photoshop. They trust us and we are across their entire enterprise stack. So that's really what's been developing over the last few months. >> Okay. I mean, I've seen in the keynote, we've seen uh the new CX Enterprise announcement. Lots of layers, lots of apps, but everything works well one with each other. Um I went to some some demos, some boots, and seen what Home Depot is talking about. >> It's pretty impressive. Now, to reach a point where it works, where it is commercially available because okay, AI, Chad GPD came out, well, went mainstream at the end of 2022. Since then, how has things evolved? How are all of these companies training their well their own IP and how is it safe, let's say, for them to use it commercially? >> I'm glad you stopped by the foundry Home Depot um demonstration. Also, we did something. I don't know if you saw the minisphere that we also had on the floor. That's um work with our own customer zero plus Foundry. And so that's an also an an interesting part when you ask about how are enterprises working to keep their IP protected but also move ahead. This is exactly why we brought Foundry to market. So if you think about the the spectrum of customization that you could have our GA models are completely uh commercially safe because originally we have licensed all the data that went in. So that's GA that's available in all the tools and it's a feature not a bug that it does not know your logo your IP your brand but that becomes a limitation when you want to work at scale and so that's a couple years ago we launched Firefly custom models which was a which was a lighter customization for image one concept at a time now that we're in the world of you like kind of brand universes building that is what foundry does across modalities and so we're not just stuck in image, we're not just stuck in video. We combine all those modalities. That's where also, you know, the market is moving to omni models that are multimod multal aware. And because we start from a base that is clean pixels for training and then we have your IP that you own and have license to when we overfit the model and train, you end up with essentially a foundational model that's protected and yours. And so large brands who really care about multiple franchises, multiple brands, they want to invest in this work because that is an asset that they need for the future. >> Okay, fair enough. Can you if you can can you walk me through the road map of let's say you go to a new enterprise and they want to adopt it? >> Yeah. >> How about what's the step by step? So we we take a very use case approach discovery for this because um our belief, you know, is that you don't just want to put technology at a problem. You want to make sure you're lined up with the most important problems that a business might have. A media and entertainment for instance that might have. And then we do that discovery work. So we think of everything we go into from a foundry perspective. um mostly like a managed service where we forward deploy engineers uh thinkers, we sit research side by side and then we solve the problem. So we would probably go in you know do some workshopping with both sides of the house. We'd really narrow in then on the the biggest use cases that move the needle for that business. In media and entertainment it probably starts with original content. So, if you think about, you know, uh, one minute of YouTube video for shoulder content might be $20,000 per minute. A minute of Netflix video is probably in the hundreds of thousands of dollars per minute. And of course, theatrical releases in a theater would be a million dollars per minute. So, a lot of the work what we're doing is trying to help those enterprises think about where where they could get more ideation. Maybe that's upfront in the previsualization process. More ideas are better upfront. And then obviously in some of the process around visual effects uh whether it's for commercial or for theatrical how can we how can AI enable them to do that work with higher quality and stay on schedule. >> I want to touch on on one of those points because I think and I've spoken with a couple of people. I think we are going to a world where there's going to be a need for more content. Maybe it's because people just spend more time on their phones and on on reals, Instagram, you name it, or on Netflix. um how how does a company deal with the fact that they need like you said they need to to create new things they need to come up with new ideas of course AI can help it and let's say you start with something I think um someone at NBC talked about they took um the NFL no the NBA >> assets that they had and then it trained it for the Olympics >> quick easy new assets were created But how can they make sure that the assets keep being original, still safe, but original and not very repetitive and boring? Because I mean, I've played around with multiple uh AI models and sometimes it's like, okay, it's cute for the first five. But then the next 50 are like, okay, this doesn't make any sense anymore or it becomes super repetitive. So, how do you balance the creative aspect and the one that's like, uh, maybe it's not that controlled anymore? >> There were like 12 questions in that. So I'm going to try to remember. You remind me. You remind me. Um, so the first thing I think is what you're describing is a math problem, right? It is true. I think McKenzie had a study that said we are now up to watching almost 13 hours of video a day on various formats. >> It's good for me. People on YouTube hopefully are watching. >> It's good for you. And I I think it's only plateaued this this this past year. My theory is because we have to eat and shower, right, at some point. So like there's only a limited amount of time we can watch video. Um and then people are consuming on multiple screens maybe at the same time. Um we get tired of content also faster. So there was some studies that were shown you know David quoted from the stage uh you know you can leave content out for two weeks and that was that was kind of the Facebook model. Now if you're on Tik Tok your content decays within hours. Yeah. >> And on YouTube, maybe you have a full day, you know, two days of content before you need to have new content refreshed out there. So that is a math problem that we have, right? Because you're you're limited in time. You need to keep fresh content. As you said, it has to be original, but it has to be on brand. >> And so the work of creating um models that are fit to your brand. So found like a foundational model that understands your brand essence in NBCU's you know case maybe it's a franchise maybe it's it's a look a logo um is that you can ideulate faster and one thing I want to you know emphasize is that we or I personally believe humanity is the innovative creativity right it's not going to come from an average normative model those models can do really great variant work >> um reframing resizing, all of that work is great. It's not necessarily going to come up, as you said, in your own experience with net new breakout creative ideas. That's where we come in, right? Directing that work. And um we launched recently our agentic creative AI assistant, which is very clearly needs to be directed by someone who has an idea, right? A great creative idea. So, that's kind of how we're working. Um, most creative producers, directors, they have so many ideas that they can't get to. So, you talk to, you know, an artist, they'll say, "I have a hundred ideas in my sketchbook. I don't have time to unlock all those ideas. I can only focus on four of them." And when you limit creativity in that way, sometimes you don't get to an optimal outcome. So the way I look at it is if you can give them tools that accelerate their ability to ideulate that's going to let us have better stories and narratives out there. So that's the way you can think about how everyone's embracing the tools to start with. >> Okay. So it seems like the human is still a pretty important part of the flywheel. I I mean in in the creativity space and and actually I I studied computer science back in the day and yes perhaps every class at MIT I would not have had to take in this world but the the the fundamental skill of problem solving and parsing problems needs to still be in our hands. Same thing with creativity. I think it's the same answer. uh before we end um where do we go from here? Because I mean I think things are already pretty advanced but it seems like every year something else comes up and like okay didn't think about that but it's pretty pretty cool. So now that we have all of these tools humans still important where do we go from here? >> I honestly think we have just started you know in baseball we're in the early innings of this game and it could be going into overtime. Um I don't think we have seen the market settle out. We're again we're exploring. You can see some leaders in the market are scaling their use cases but many companies have yet to figure out how to move from experimentation into scale. So I think we're going to see a huge ramp in adoption once the unlocks of where are the ROIs back to use cases. Why I focus so much on use cases. Where is the opportunity and where does this technology unlock? That's where I think we're going. Of course, we're going to talk about the models will get better, they'll get cheaper, the quality will be higher. um agents will start to stitch and make sure you listen to keynote day two because there'll be a lot of thinking around >> where we used to have to work in three or four different surfaces having an agent that is has access to the data in a safe way and a protected way but also understands the workflow that needs to get done is also going to speed up our lives. So I think that's where we're headed um for the future. >> Now that we talk about the agents I I have to talk about the agents. So um if you can share something where I mean how big of a big deal is this new shift towards agentic cuz like you said I mean me personally I don't like to work on 10 different platforms. So >> I assume maybe these new platforms are becoming the the new UI where we do work and everything's integrated and connected. Is that going to make sure that the content does become 10 times more I mean there's going to be 10 times more output quality is still there but agents are going to soal control or be be the ones that that do most of the work. >> I borrowed this phrase from the head of um marketing ops at Home Depot. She said Stacy she said um you can't AI your way out of a bad process. Okay. So, Agentic is like the PhD level class in making workflows better. But if you have poor workflows now or you have, you know, 12 different steps to do something, it's the perfect moment to step back and say, is that the workflow of the future that I need for whatever I'm doing, making a podcast, marketing the podcast, and where can AI really help me unlock different pieces? I think once you understand the workflow now you can put an agent to work and you know yesterday at uh because we have NAB here as well the NVIDIA team asked me to come and speak on a panel there and we talked a lot about governance for Agentic and observability so you need to be able to measure whether the agents being effective. I would not recommend to anyone just to set an agent off in a workflow you don't understand or haven't debugged essentially. So I this is where it's the you know it's always the hot new word that that people want to go for. That's the PhD level first. Make sure that your workflows are in a good place. You know where your data is and that's all governed really well. >> Okay. Fair enough. Good way good way to end I think. Um thanks again for coming on. Really appreciate it. Probably the next time we we will talk we'll talk about some new things that AI uh or and human and human have created together. Um so thanks again for coming. Tomorrow we will have a meet so I'm going to talk about the more enterprise CX enterprise experience more platform dashboards AI agents every every hot topic today so um it's going to be exciting so thanks again >> thank you for having me >> and we're back this time with a meet SVP of products at Adobe welcome thanks for taking the time >> absolutely my pleasure thanks for uh thanks for having me appreciate >> it's been good start of the day you just gave a very nice uh speech and introduction of new products that Adobe is launching Yeah. >> Um can you first talk about what's your role at Adobe? >> Yeah. Yeah. No, I'm happy to. Um so a little bit of history. I've been with Adobe about 21 years. Um in many different roles, but for the last uh many years I am responsible for product management and product marketing for what we call CXO, customer experience orchestration. So a lot of the enterprise offerings that people are aware of, but my job is to think about product roadmap and and product marketing. >> Okay. We spoke a little bit about uh about the fact that Adobe can be split into two parts. one the part where I think most the viewers know about Photoshop, Premiere Pro, etc. And then the other part is the part where >> it's it's a big business, the enterprise business, marketing, analytics, it's a big business. And I do think that now with AI agents, with AI in general, >> yes, >> it's becoming bigger and bigger and much more important. Now, >> you made a couple of big announcements recently this week. Y >> new partnerships with uh big companies with companies like Entropic, OpenAI, Nvidia. >> Y >> why why are we going into that direction where now things seem to be more open? We've seen also another company Salesforce do approximately the same with Headless 360. So >> software companies are opening up their ecosystem it seems. Um what's the reasoning behind it? >> Yeah. Well, I I first of all I think it's a it's a great question. I think maybe I can give a little bit of context on on a couple things, right, that you that you said. I think number one is I think you're you're right. Uh most most people are more familiar with some other sides of Adobe on this side. Um we actually have a lot of um we're very uh proud of kind of what we've done because if you rewind the clock and I don't know if you saw the day one keynote, but I think we really came out many years ago and defined what is digital marketing, right? as a category. Many years later, we defined what we call customer experience management, which is a broadening of depth and capability. And then last year, we unleashed uh and announced this customer experience orchestration. And so, I think there's a whole history of how we have been effectively leading out in this market and kind of bringing what we think are the right tools together to help to help our customers. Um, and then to your point around the uh ecosystem, which is a really good question. Look, it's really interesting and and um even if you rewind the clock and I've had many many uh summits behind me now, which has been really fun. Even if you rewind the clock back several years, we've always kind of really focused on the ecosystem. So, and but historically, I think where you're pushing on historically, the ecosystem was mostly around kind of three constituents, right? It was mostly around, hey, how are we working with we call ISVS, basically large tech providers, um system integrators and agencies, right? and we've had years of working with them very deeply. Um, and it's been very very successful for I think all of us in this ecosystem. I think the change this year which I think is what you're alluding to is that now there's a whole new wave of kind of AI platforms that we are absolutely integrating with. And to answer your question, our ecosystem and our northstar is always effectively directed by our customers, right? And that's what I really like. They are growing and uh getting a larger share in the advertising space. I've got shares in Micron, and that's why uh and and a lot of shares in all the different uh AI names. So, it, you know, SanDisk is going to be very important for Micron. It's going to be very important for AMD and Nvidia and many of the data data center spaces to understand how much they're paying for a lot of this uh a lot of the the chip build-outs. As Jose was saying earlier on our live stream together is that he stated that, you know, a large percentage what what's driving the most amount of CapEx growth this year is not new data centers that uh these companies are finding that they want to build out, but just that the cost of the data center is going up, and so CapEx is going to go up. Look at this red candle. Uh SanDisk just lost 10 $10 a share. You know what's so funny is before this pump, because this stock, if you were to look back 1 year, we're up 34 times. 34 times our money right now on SanDisk. Now, obviously, it's extremely rare to find an an investment that compounds the way that SanDisk has, where in a year, you end up finding those 33, 34x returns. You know, I I I can't think of another time where I've seen it. Um but that compounding effect of the the the stock moves today versus your original cost basis, nowhere near, right? With that, I'll turn the call over to David. Thanks, Ivan. Good afternoon and thank you for joining SanDisk's fiscal third quarter earnings call. We delivered another strong quarter with excellent performance across all key metrics, reflecting the strength of the SanDisk franchise. Before turning to our end markets, I'd like to provide an update on a priority we previously outlined. Last quarter, we were engaged in discussions with customers on multi-year supply partnerships, what we referred to as new business models, or NBMs. I am pleased to share that we have successfully advanced those conversations with five multi-year partnerships signed so far. These partnerships are structured to lock in committed supply for our customers and committed financials for SanDisk. Our customers' commitments are backed by firm financial guarantees. These partnerships support durable, structurally higher earnings, and a significantly more predictable and less cyclical business for SanDisk. We believe this marks a fundamental evolution of our business, which is centered on deeper customer alignment, enhanced visibility, and long-term value creation. These NBMs reflect the strategic value of our world-class NAND technology, which is built on decades of innovation. The investment we've made in R&D and manufacturing, including tens of billions of dollars in cumulative capex and IP, have built the foundation for a powerful new business model in which we manage the full stack, from front-end manufacturing through chip and system-level design to final back-end assembly and test. Both the extension of our joint venture with Kioxia and the supply agreement for DRAM following our investment in Nanya further strengthen our supply chain resiliency. This leverage is enabling us to drive stronger customer engagement, allowing long-term conversations with partners who value technology performance and long-term supply assurance. With increased engagement and the optionality across the portfolio, we can optimize our end market mix more effectively. Together, these transformations have resulted in a step change in what we believe to be sustainable gross margins, free cash flow generation, and earnings power in a market that we expect to grow in the double digits for the foreseeable future. Data center is a clear example of this strategy in action with revenue growing 233% sequentially. This milestone reflects years of preparation and our deliberate shift toward what is now the most strategic and fastest-growing end market. While we have made substantial progress, there are significant growth opportunities ahead driven by the fundamental shift in underlying infrastructure requirements of artificial intelligence. We are witnessing extraordinary growth, not just in model size, but in resulting token generation, the duration and complexity of model runs, and the increasing importance of context. As AI models scale from billions to trillions of parameters, and deployments advance from simple inference to deep reasoning in increasingly autonomous agentic systems, NAND has become a critical component of the underlying infrastructure. Inference optimizations, such as KB cache, along with workloads like Rag, require substantial high-performance, low-latency flash to deliver real-time responsiveness and quality of user experience. These workloads expand the amount of data that now needs to be stored on low latency flash, which is well beyond the model itself. As systems must retain context, intermediate data, and large external data sets. As a result, NAND flash is emerging as the only economically viable solution to deliver that capacity, performance, and efficiency required to keep models accessible for real-time inference at scale. This shift in understanding the critical nature of our technology comes at a time when our product differentiation is strongest. Anchored in what has been recognized as an industry gold standard for NAND technology with BiCS 8, and a broad leading portfolio with TLC and QLC offerings. We are confident that our world-class product portfolio and technology leadership will continue to drive data center customers to see SanDisk as a partner of choice over the long term. And we are already seeing that preference translate into results. Our fiscal third quarter revenue was enhanced by strong demand for our TLC-based enterprise SSD portfolio, which powers performance-intensive compute workloads where speed and latency are paramount. Looking ahead to the fiscal fourth quarter, we expect to begin shipping our QLC Stargate solutions for revenue, adding another layer of revenue growth. Together, TLC and QLC serve distinct but complementary roles, reflecting how we are deliberately architecting our portfolio to meet evolving customer needs with our broad portfolio of AI-focused data center products. In edge, we are seeing a continued shift towards premium devices across both PC and smartphone markets. These platforms are increasingly incorporating on-device capabilities, which are driving higher storage requirements, and greater demand for high-performance solutions. As a result, our mix continues to shift to high-value configuration and customers that assign the appropriate value to our technology. Consumers saw strong year-over-year revenue growth across all key storage categories and regions, despite evolving consumer industry dynamics. This performance was supported by our strong brand recognition and channel presence as we focused on the most financially attractive demand. In February, we unveiled our next-generation portable SSD portfolio, designed to support faster, more demanding workflows and AI-enabled content creation. This launch reinforced our innovation and leadership in the SSD category, generating meaningful external visibility with coverage across multiple global media outlets. We also continued to strengthen global consumer engagement through new brand-led go-to-market activities such as our Space to Hold More campaign, which is driving deeper customer connection by localizing global narratives and engaging diverse communities worldwide. Together, these efforts reflect our focus on our end markets and commitment to driving demand through brand recognition, product innovation, and strong go-to-market execution as we shift our portfolio toward higher value opportunities and transition away from legacy upsell models. Our broad end market exposure sets us apart, and we remain committed to serving customers across these markets. With that, I'll turn the call over to Louis to dive deeper into our financial performance and guidance. Thank you, David. I will begin with an update on our new business models, or NBMs, which are designed to provide us with demand certainty and provide our customers with supply assurance. We signed three agreements in the third quarter and an additional two so far in the fourth quarter, and we're currently in active negotiations with several other customers. These agreements are tailored to meet the needs of our customers and in aggregate provide us with demand certainty at financials that we expect will be consistent with our fiscal fourth quarter guidance. The duration of these agreements varies, with the longest contract extending to 5 years. In aggregate, volume commitments increased during the life of the contracts with quarterly commitments and a combination of fixed and variable pricing. These agreements with variable pricing allow us to capture upside if prices rise, while allowing our customers some upside if prices decline over time. As you will see in our 10-Q, the three contracts signed during the quarter provide minimum contractual revenue of approximately $42 billion. We will update you as we make more progress. Each contract is secured with financial guarantees that protect us if the purchase obligations are not fully performed by our customers. In aggregate, the five agreements signed so far include financial guarantees that exceed $11 billion and include prepayments and other financial instruments managed by third-party financial institutions. Out of these agreements, 0.4 billion dollars in prepayments are included in our Q3 balance sheet. These five new business models account for over a third of our bits in fiscal year 2027, which we expect to increase as we conclude additional agreements over the next few months. We expect these new business models to reshape the historical cyclicality of our business, improving visibility and resulting in pricing and margins that reflect the value of our technology and investments, ultimately delivering higher, more consistent, and durable returns for shareholders. Moving on to our results for the quarter, revenue for the third quarter was $5,950 million, up 97% sequentially, and up 251% year-over-year. This compares favorably to our guidance of $4,400 to $4,800 million, and was driven by both a mix shift towards higher value customers and higher pricing. Our bits shipments were flat year-over-year and down high teens sequentially as we built higher inventory levels, primarily to support strong BiCS 8 QLC demand in the fourth quarter, Stargate ramp, and to prepare for our recently signed new business models. In line with our mid-to-high teens growth model, bits shipments increased 18% fiscal year-to-date. Moving on to the end markets, sequentially, data center revenue grew 233% to $1,467 million, edge grew 118% to $3,663 million, and consumer came in at $820 million, down 10% in line with our historical seasonality. Our portfolio planning strategy focuses on delivering attractive long-term economics with diversification remaining a core strength. We remain committed to serving all three end markets to maximize long-term value creation. Our non-GAAP gross margin for the third quarter was 78.4% up from 51.1% in the prior quarter. This compares favorably to our guidance of 65 to 67% and was driven by our shift towards higher value mix and the overall pricing environment. Non-GAAP operating expenses for the third quarter were $448 million and represent 7.5% of revenue as compared to 13.7% of revenue in the prior quarter as we generate additional leverage. This compares favorably to our guidance range of $450 to $470 million. As a result, non-GAAP operating margin were 70.9% up from 37.5% in the prior quarter. Non-GAAP EPS was $23.41 up from $6.20 in the prior quarter. This compares favorably to our guidance range of $12 to $14. Key GAAP to non-GAAP reconciliation items include $20 million in stock-based compensation, net of taxes, which represents 0.3% of revenue, and $46 million related to the write-off of unamortized issuance fees as a result of our prepayment of the remaining $650 million balance in our TLB. We closed the quarter with $3,735 million in cash and cash equivalents on our balance sheet. Moving on to free cash flow, during the quarter, we generated $2,955 million in adjusted free cash flow, which represents a 49.7% margin. Cash flow from operations came in at $3,038 million, partially offset by $83 million from net cash capital spending. Gross capital expenditures totaled $240 million and represented 4% of revenue. Our capital plan is designed to balance growth opportunities and generate attractive returns while supporting our ongoing BiCS 8 transition. We remain highly disciplined in how we evaluate such investments to protect long-term sustainability of our business financials. Moving on to guidance. For the fourth quarter, we forecast revenue between 7,750 million to 8,250 million dollars from both bits growth and higher pricing. Our forecast for non-GAAP gross margin is between 79% and 81%. We expect non-GAAP operating expenses between 480 and 500 million dollars as we continue to invest in innovation and R&D. We expect non-GAAP interest and other income between 10 and 30 million dollars and non-GAAP tax expenses between 775 and 875 million dollars. We forecast non-GAAP EPS between 30 and 33 dollars assuming 158 million fully diluted shares. Moving to capital allocation. The priorities we outlined in February of last year were to invest in the business, achieve a net cash position, and then return cash to shareholders. In line with these priorities, we have taken steps over the last two quarters to solidify our supply chain including extending our JV with Kioxia through December 2034 and investing approximately 1 billion dollars in YMTC to secure long-term DRAM supply. We have also taken actions that put us in a strong net cash position by paying off the remaining balance of our TLB. Given the strong progress, today we're announcing that our board of directors has authorized a 6 billion dollar share buyback program of outstanding shares of common stock. The repurchase authorization is effective immediately with no expiration date. With that, I'll turn the call back to David for closing remarks. Thank you, Louis. In summary, we continue to execute with conviction at a critical inflection point for this business. NAND has always been a foundational technology. Empowering the world's best-in-class semiconductor storage solutions required to drive the largest technological movements including PC, mobile, cloud, and now artificial intelligence. Data center has become our fastest growing market. And the workloads driving that demand including inference, reasoning, and agentic systems represent a structural and durable shift in how the world's most consequential technology is built and deployed. Our new business models reflect this shift. Five signed agreements to date, over 11 billion dollars in financial guarantees, and over a third of our bits in fiscal year 2027 under firm customer commitments represent a fundamental reshaping of our business providing visibility, pricing protection, and more consistent durable returns. Our technology and product portfolio are intersecting this extraordinary demand at exactly the right moment. Equipped with a complete portfolio that now includes a scaled and rapidly growing enterprise SSD business, we're allocating supply to the highest value opportunities and establishing a new pillar of growth for SanDisk. This progress has converged in a single moment. We believe our margins are sustainable, we've achieved our net cash target, and we've announced plans to return capital to shareholders through buybacks, all while reinforcing our operational foundation. Combined with our multi-year NVMs and the acceleration in the data center end market, this gives us both financial strength and structural resilience. The result is a durable growth model, a valuable franchise, and a business built to generate substantial sustained cash flow. With that, Ivan, let's see if there's any questions. Thank you. We will now begin the question and answer session. To ask a question, you press star then one on your telephone keypad. If [clears throat] you're using a speakerphone, please pick up your handset before pressing the keys. If at any time your question has been addressed and you would like to withdraw your question, please press star then two. At this time, we will pause momentarily to assemble our roster. The first question comes from Mark Newman with Bernstein. Please go ahead. Thanks very much for taking my question and congrats on another great quarter. Um couple couple of just quick questions here. So, the uh EPS guidance you've given uh 30 dollars to 33 dollars and these these are all fun these are all fantastic numbers. Um it does imply that the rate of price increase is um slowing a bit into the um the current quarter. And I just wondered if that is either um being conservative on your side cuz obviously we're still quite early in the quarter or is that um related to some of these very very long-term agreements um that you've signed? Um and with regards to the long-term agreements, I believe you mentioned about a third of bits for FY27 uh in some kind of long-term agreement. I I'd like to ask um to what degree is a price uh fixed in in in in in coming quarters just so I can get a kind of sense for that. Thank you very much. Really appreciate it. Hey, Mark. Uh it's good to hear from you and thanks for the comments. So, uh first on next quarter and pricing, so we don't really guide pricing, but I think you saw in FQ3 rather extraordinary uh pricing acceleration across the business. Um so, we're we're very happy about that. Um and you're right, it's early in the quarter and it's an extremely dynamic market. So, um you know, it pays to be uh it pays to to to be a bit conservative when you're when you're going down that path, but you know, we're very confident in the numbers. Um the second part on the agreements, I will uh I'll make a few comments and and Louis will have something to say as well. Uh I think you were asking about uh pricing being fixed. So, these these these agreements are really tailored to individual customers. So, they have different elements um depending on the customer. It depends on the length of the agreement. It really gives us um you know, some assurance on consistency of demand which is really what we need. I mean again, a lot of this is I've talked about this in the past. We need to get our customers' business model and our business model aligned. We run a fab. We have very consistent output. We need very consistent consumption. And uh you know, I think the the the primary thing one of the one of the major attributes of these agreements is they give us that. And our customers have uh they understand the dynamic uh very clearly when we talk it through. It's one of the reasons why these agreements don't just happen overnight. It's not just about prepaying for a couple quarters' worth of supply. This is about establishing a you know, up to a five-year agreement on supply that's very consistent quarter over quarter. And uh you know, as we said, there's there's financial instruments in place that if uh if that consumption does not happen on that very predictable time frame, then there's uh there's financial commitments that come to us immediately. So, they are backed up and they're very very strong. Um the pricing, like I said, it's set up where there's there's fixed elements, there's variable elements. Maybe I'll let Louis talk about it in a little more detail. Yeah, I just want to reinforce what David was saying. These models are here to deliver durable, more predictable, more attractive, more consistent financial results. So, they are they're
